title: The Quidditch World Cup
prose: The summer before fourth year has exactly one topic of conversation: the Quidditch World Cup Final, Ireland against Bulgaria, held on a stretch of Muggle-repelled moorland packed with more wizards and witches than you've ever seen in one place. The match itself is a blur — the Irish Chasers moving almost too fast to follow, and Bulgaria's Viktor Krum catching the Snitch in a dive that wins his side nothing but the crowd's awe anyway, since Ireland's already won on points.

The celebration afterward doesn't last. Deep in the night, screams start spreading through the campsite — a crowd of masked figures in dark robes, torching tents, and above it all, something vast and green and skull-shaped burning into the night sky. The Dark Mark. You've never seen real panic before that night; you have now, and the memory of that green skull stays considerably longer than the memory of the match.

Ministry officials swarm the site by dawn, and the story that filters out over the following days is careful and incomplete in the way official stories often are — but everyone who was there agrees on one thing: whoever conjured that Mark wanted it seen, and wanted people to be afraid.

choices.label: The Mark in the sky is the part that stays with you.
You try to hold onto the memory of the match instead.
